Learn how to use CommandModule and Comma: Learn how to use CommandModule and CommandService for publishing and managing commands.
The `CommandService` is a core component: The `CommandService` is a core component of the framework that facilitates the management and synchronization of commands. It primarily provides methods for publishing both full commands and partial commands, allowing for their processing either synchronously or asynchronously, thereby enhancing the overall efficiency and flexibility of command handling within the system.
The `CommandModule` is a dynamic module: The `CommandModule` is a dynamic module used to register data sync handlers and provide services associated with a table name. When importing this module, you must provide a specific option for use.
Reserved for future use. Not yet impleme: Reserved for future use. Not yet implemented.
If set to `true`, disables the default D: If set to `true`, disables the default DynamoDB data sync handler
Here, the `CommandModule` registers with: Here, the `CommandModule` registers with the `cat` table name and provides the `CatDataSyncRdsHandler` to the data sync handlers.
In the example for the method below, ass: In the example for the method below, assume you import the `CommandModule` into your module as follows:
Then, the `CommandService` and `DataServ: Then, the `CommandService` and `DataService` will be ready for injection into other services for your use.
For complete CRUD implementation pattern: For complete CRUD implementation patterns using CommandService, see [Service Patterns](./service-patterns.md).
Utilize this method to publish a full co: Utilize this method to publish a full command, as it will insert the command data into the **command** table.
The method provides immediate feedback b: The method provides immediate feedback by returning the command data right away, allowing you to proceed without waiting for the command to be processed. Subsequently, the command is handled asynchronously in the background, ensuring that your application remains responsive while the processing occurs.
**Return Value:** Returns `Promise<Comma: **Return Value:** Returns `Promise<CommandModel>` on success, or `Promise<null>` when the command is not dirty (no changes detected compared to the existing command).
For example, you can publish a new cat c: For example, you can publish a new cat command as bellow:
This method allows you to create new com: This method allows you to create new command data based on the previous command with the same `pk` and `sk` (primary key) values.
As same as the `publishAsync` method, th: As same as the `publishAsync` method, the method immediately returns the updated command data without waiting for the command to be processed.
For example, you want to update cat's na: For example, you want to update cat's name:
*async* `publishSync( input: CommandInpu: *async* `publishSync( input: CommandInputModel, options: ICommandOptions): Promise\<CommandModel | null\>`
This method serves as a synchronous coun: This method serves as a synchronous counterpart to the `publishAsync` method, meaning that it will halt the execution of the code until the command has been fully processed. This ensures that you receive the result of the command before proceeding with any further operations in your code.
Since [v1.2.0](/docs/changelog#v120), `p: Since [v1.2.0](/docs/changelog#v120), `publishSync()` and `publishPartialUpdateSync()` return `null` when the command is not dirty (no-op). Always null-check the result before accessing properties:
no-op: command was not dirty, nothing wa: no-op: command was not dirty, nothing was written
Since [v1.1.4](/docs/changelog#v114), `p: Since [v1.1.4](/docs/changelog#v114), `publishSync` writes a full audit trail matching the async pipeline:
An immutable event is written to the Com: An immutable event is written to the Command table with `syncMode: 'SYNC'` marker
The History table is populated, providin: The History table is populated, providing complete event sourcing parity
Returns `null` when the command is not d: Returns `null` when the command is not dirty (no-op), matching `publishAsync` behavior
DynamoDB Stream filter excludes `syncMod: DynamoDB Stream filter excludes `syncMode=SYNC` records to prevent Step Functions double-execution
Prior to v1.1.4, `publishSync` bypassed: Prior to v1.1.4, `publishSync` bypassed the Command table to avoid triggering Step Functions, resulting in a missing audit trail and no History table entries.
Returns `null` if no changes are detecte: Returns `null` if no changes are detected (dirty check optimization).
*async* `publishPartialUpdateSync( input: *async* `publishPartialUpdateSync( input: CommandPartialInputModel, options: ICommandOptions): Promise\<CommandModel | null\>`
This method is a synchronous version of: This method is a synchronous version of the `publishPartialUpdateAsync` method. It will block the execution of the code until the command is processed.

This method requires the `version` field: This method requires the `version` field in the input to match the current version of the existing item. If the item is not found or the version does not match, a `BadRequestException` is thrown with the message "The input is not a valid, item not found or version not match".

This method allows you to create new com: This method allows you to create new command data based on the previous command.

If you want to reapply the data sync han: If you want to reapply the data sync handler, this method is designed for you to use. You only need to call the function as follows:
Retrieves a command item by its primary: Retrieves a command item by its primary key. If the sort key does not include a version separator, it automatically calls `getLatestItem` to get the latest version.
Get a specific version of a command: Get a specific version of a command
If no version in sk, automatically gets: If no version in sk, automatically gets latest version
Retrieves the latest version of a comman: Retrieves the latest version of a command item by its primary key. This method uses a lookup algorithm that starts from the data table's version and searches up/down to find the most recent command version.
Retrieves the next version of a command: Retrieves the next version of a command based on the current command's key. This is useful for processing command chains or implementing retry logic.
Returns command with sk: \"CAT#cat001@3\: Returns command with sk: "CAT#cat001@3" if exists
*async* `updateStatus(key: DetailKey, st: *async* `updateStatus(key: DetailKey, status: string, notifyId?: string): Promise<void>`
Updates the status of a command and send: Updates the status of a command and sends an SNS notification. This is commonly used to update task or process statuses and notify subscribers of the change.
Update status and send SNS notification: Update status and send SNS notification
Creates a duplicate of an existing comma: Creates a duplicate of an existing command with an incremented version number. The duplicated command will have `source` set to `"duplicated"` and updated metadata (timestamp, user, IP).
Stores an AWS Step Functions task token: Stores an AWS Step Functions task token on a command item. This is used when integrating with Step Functions to enable callback patterns.
Store the Step Functions task token: Store the Step Functions task token
Later, use the token to send task succes: Later, use the token to send task success/failure
Updates the TTL (Time To Live) of the pr: Updates the TTL (Time To Live) of the previous version of a command. This is typically used internally to manage command history retention. Returns `null` if the version is too low or the previous command doesn't exist.
This method is primarily used internally: This method is primarily used internally by the framework for command history management. Direct usage is rarely needed in application code.
Returns the array of registered data syn: Returns the array of registered data sync handlers for this CommandService instance. This is useful when you need to inspect or iterate over the handlers programmatically.
Get all registered data sync handlers: Get all registered data sync handlers
Retrieves a specific data sync handler b: Retrieves a specific data sync handler by its class name. Returns `undefined` if no handler with the specified name is found.
Get a specific handler by name: Get a specific handler by name
Compares an existing command item with a: Compares an existing command item with a new input to determine if there are any actual changes. Returns `true` if the command is NOT dirty (no changes), returns `false` if there ARE changes.
This method is used internally by publis: This method is used internally by publish methods to skip unnecessary writes when no changes are detected. You can also use it directly to check if an update would result in any changes before calling publish.
Check if an update would result in chang: Check if an update would result in changes
No changes detected, skip the update: No changes detected, skip the update
Gets or sets the DynamoDB table name for: Gets or sets the DynamoDB table name for this CommandService instance. The table name is configured when registering the `CommandModule`, but can be changed at runtime if needed.
Changing the table name at runtime is an: Changing the table name at runtime is an advanced use case. In most applications, you should configure the table name through `CommandModule.register()` and not change it afterwards.
The Problem: Stale Reads After publishAs: The Problem: Stale Reads After publishAsync
`publishAsync` returns immediately after: `publishAsync` returns immediately after writing to the Command table, then the DynamoDB Stream pipeline asynchronously syncs the data to the read model (Data table). This creates a short window where a subsequent read by the same user returns stale data — for example, a "Create Order" button submits successfully, the user is redirected to the order list, but the new order does not appear yet.
RYW bridges this gap by temporarily cach: RYW bridges this gap by temporarily caching the version number of the pending command in a short-lived session table. When reading through `Repository`, the session entry is detected and the pending command is fetched from the Command table and merged into the response — making the write immediately visible to the user who made it.
**Write path** — after a successful `pub: **Write path** — after a successful `publishAsync`, `CommandService` writes a session entry to `{NODE_ENV}-{APP_NAME}-session` table:
`version`: the version number of the pub: `version`: the version number of the published command
`ttl`: current time + `RYW_SESSION_TTL_M: `ttl`: current time + `RYW_SESSION_TTL_MINUTES` seconds (DynamoDB TTL auto-deletes the entry)
**Note**: if `RYW_SESSION_TTL_MINUTES` i: **Note**: if `RYW_SESSION_TTL_MINUTES` is unset, this write is skipped entirely. `Repository` reads still work correctly — they simply find no session entries and fall back to `DataService`.
**Read path** — `Repository` checks the: **Read path** — `Repository` checks the session table before returning data:
For `getItem`: if a session entry exists: For `getItem`: if a session entry exists, fetches the exact versioned command and merges it with any existing data item
For `listItemsByPk`: fetches all session: For `listItemsByPk`: fetches all session entries for the user/tenant/module, then applies updates, deletes, and create-new prepends to the base list result
For `listItems` (external sources like R: For `listItems` (external sources like RDS): same merge logic, with `transformCommand` to convert the command shape to the external query shape. Unlike `listItemsByPk` (which derives `tenantCode` from the `pk` argument), `listItems` derives `tenantCode` from `getUserContext(options.invokeContext)` — so `options` with a valid `invokeContext` is required for RYW to activate
**Session expiry** — entries expire auto: **Session expiry** — entries expire automatically via DynamoDB TTL. Once the Stream sync completes (typically 1–3 seconds), the session entry is redundant and will be cleaned up within the TTL window.
Proactive RYW session cleanup and the `g: Proactive RYW session cleanup and the `getVersion` short-circuit were added in [v1.2.6](/docs/changelog#v126).
Prior to v1.2.6, RYW sessions persisted: Prior to v1.2.6, RYW sessions persisted until their TTL expired, even after the data table had absorbed the corresponding write. This created a "stale override" window: if another user or external system updated the same item to a newer version, the session would still cause the originating user to see their own older command merged on top of the data table — effectively reverting visible state until the TTL elapsed.
Since v1.2.6, `Repository` actively purg: Since v1.2.6, `Repository` actively purges sessions in the background once the data table version meets or exceeds the session version. This guarantees that:
The originating user immediately sees th: The originating user immediately sees the latest data once the Stream sync completes — no more stale-override window
Subsequent reads skip the unnecessary co: Subsequent reads skip the unnecessary command-table lookup, reducing latency
The session table stays small even when: The session table stays small even when the configured TTL is generous
Cleanup is **fire-and-forget** — failure: Cleanup is **fire-and-forget** — failures are logged as warnings (`Failed to delete RYW session (non-fatal): ...`) and never block the read path. All behavior is automatic when `RYW_SESSION_TTL_MINUTES` is enabled; no application code changes are required.
Optional: `getVersion` short-circuit for: Optional: `getVersion` short-circuit for RDS read models
`Repository.listItems()` (the RDS path): `Repository.listItems()` (the RDS path) now accepts an optional `mergeOptions.getVersion` callback. If your RDS rows already carry a `version` column, supplying this callback lets the merge loop skip the extra DynamoDB GetItem when the existing RDS row already proves caught-up:
map other CommandModel fields to your RD: map other CommandModel fields to your RDS row shape
**Note:** `getVersion` only short-circui: **Note:** `getVersion` only short-circuits the **update path** (when the session's `itemId` matches an existing RDS row). Create-new items (session present but not yet reflected in RDS) still fetch the command as before — there is no existing row to derive a version from.
Set `RYW_SESSION_TTL_MINUTES` to a posit: Set `RYW_SESSION_TTL_MINUTES` to a positive integer. A value of `5` (minutes) is a safe default — it covers any Stream sync delay while keeping the session table small.
If unset (or set to 0 or a non-number),: If unset (or set to 0 or a non-number), the feature is completely disabled: session writes are skipped and `Repository` behaves identically to `DataService`.
Step 2: Create the session DynamoDB tabl: Step 2: Create the session DynamoDB table
Add `dynamodbs/session.json` to your pro: Add `dynamodbs/session.json` to your project (same location as your other table definitions):
The table name is automatically computed: The table name is automatically computed as `{NODE_ENV}-{APP_NAME}-session` (e.g. `dev-myapp-session`).
`Repository` is a drop-in replacement fo: `Repository` is a drop-in replacement for `DataService` for read operations that need RYW consistency. It is exported from `@mbc-cqrs-serverless/core` (via `CommandModule`). Register it in your module and inject it in your service.
`Repository` is automatically provided a: `Repository` is automatically provided and exported by `CommandModule.register()` — no extra configuration is needed.
If the user just published an async comm: If the user just published an async command for this item,
the pending version is merged in — the u: the pending version is merged in — the user always sees their own write.
Pass `{ latestFlg: true }` as the third: Pass `{ latestFlg: true }` as the third argument to enable merging. If `mergeOptions` is omitted or `latestFlg` is `false`/`undefined`, the method behaves identically to `DataService.listItemsByPk`.
Prepended to the top of the list (sorted: Prepended to the top of the list (sorted by `updatedAt` desc among multiple pending creates)
Replaced in-place — original sort positi: Replaced in-place — original sort position preserved
Newly created items are prepended to the: Newly created items are prepended to the top of the list, not integrated into the caller's sort order (e.g. by `name` or `code`). They appear at the top until the DynamoDB Stream sync completes and the next read returns fully sorted data. This is intentional — RYW guarantees *visibility*, not sort position.
`listItems` — external source (RDS / Ela: `listItems` — external source (RDS / Elasticsearch) with RYW merge
For services that query an external sour: For services that query an external source (e.g. RDS via TypeORM or Prisma), use `listItems` with a `transformCommand` function to convert the pending command into the same shape as your query result.
Convert a pending CommandModel into an O: Convert a pending CommandModel into an OrderDto
carry over join fields that don't exist: carry over join fields that don't exist in the command
Only prepend create-new items that match: Only prepend create-new items that match the current search filter
The base query — runs as normal: The base query — runs as normal
`total` is adjusted after merge (increme: `total` is adjusted after merge (incremented for create-new, decremented for delete). However, prepended items sit outside the RDS `LIMIT`/`OFFSET` window, so on page 2+ there may be a one-item overlap or gap until the Stream sync completes. For most UX patterns (redirect-after-write, optimistic UI) this is unnoticeable.
Returns fresh data (session expired or n: Returns fresh data (session expired or not found)
Returns list with pending item prepended: Returns list with pending item prepended
1–2 extra DynamoDB reads per pending ite: 1–2 extra DynamoDB reads per pending item
**Visibility guarantee, not ordering**:: **Visibility guarantee, not ordering**: Pending create-new items appear at the top of lists, not in the caller's sort order.
**Single-user scope**: RYW only applies: **Single-user scope**: RYW only applies to the user who published the command. Other users still see the eventual-consistent read until the Stream sync completes.
**`listItems` pagination overlap**: On p: **`listItems` pagination overlap**: On paginated external queries (RDS), a create-new item may appear on both page 1 (prepended) and page 2 (after sync). This resolves on the next full reload.
**Session table cost**: Each `publishAsy: **Session table cost**: Each `publishAsync` call writes one DynamoDB item per updated entity. With `RYW_SESSION_TTL_MINUTES=5` the items are small and short-lived. Cost is negligible for typical workloads.
